algorithms.name: Generate All Subsets
algorithms.analogy: For each item, you make a binary choice: include it or exclude it. With n items, there are 2^n possible combinations of choices. A recursive tree branches at each element: left branch = include, right branch = exclude. Every leaf of this tree is a valid subset. Backtracking means you 'undo' the include choice after exploring that branch.
algorithms.story: Subsets is the mother of all backtracking problems. The template: iterate from a start index, add element to path, recurse with start+1, remove element (backtrack). This guarantees no duplicates and generates all 2^n subsets. For subsets with duplicates, sort first and skip duplicates at the same recursion level.
algorithms.whenToUse: Subsets, combinations, power set, any problem where you need all valid collections of elements without a fixed size.
algorithms.howItWorks:
Backtracking template:
1. Add current path to result
2. For i from start to n-1:
   a. Add nums[i] to path
   b. Recurse with start = i+1
   c. Remove nums[i] from path (backtrack)

Bitmask approach: for each number 0 to 2^n - 1, bit j being set means include nums[j].
algorithms.interviewTips: Add to result at EVERY recursion level (not just leaves) — that's what makes it subsets not combinations. For duplicates: sort + skip `if (i > start && nums[i] == nums[i-1])`. The `i > start` condition (not `i > 0`) is critical — it only skips duplicates at the SAME level, not across levels.
algorithms.useCases: ["Subsets I/II (LeetCode 78/90)","Power set generation","All possible groupings","Bitmask DP state spaces"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Using new ArrayList<>(path) when adding (otherwise all results point to same list)","For Subsets II: using i>0 instead of i>start (incorrectly skips valid subsets)","Forgetting to backtrack (remove last element)"]

algorithms.name: Permutations
algorithms.analogy: Arranging n distinct books on a shelf. For the first slot, pick any of n books. For the second slot, any of the remaining n-1. And so on. Total arrangements: n! The backtracking tree has depth n, and each level has one fewer choice. A boolean visited[] array tracks which elements have been used in the current path.
algorithms.story: Permutations differ from subsets in two ways: order matters, and you use all elements. The backtracking approach: iterate over ALL elements at each level (not from start), but skip already-used ones. A visited array or swap-based approach avoids using a set. For duplicates: sort + skip if visited[i-1] is false and nums[i] == nums[i-1].
algorithms.whenToUse: Generate all orderings, letter combinations, all arrangements of elements, next permutation problems.
algorithms.howItWorks:
Using visited array:
1. For each index i from 0 to n-1:
   a. If visited[i] → skip
   b. Mark visited[i] = true, add nums[i] to path
   c. Recurse
   d. Mark visited[i] = false, remove nums[i] (backtrack)
2. If path.size() == n → add to result
algorithms.interviewTips: The duplicate-skipping condition `i > 0 && nums[i] == nums[i-1] && !visited[i-1]` is subtle. It means: skip if same value as previous AND previous wasn't used in this path (meaning we're in a new branch at the same level, not a deeper level). Internalize this or you'll get wrong answers with duplicates.
algorithms.useCases: ["Permutations I/II (LeetCode 46/47)","Letter combinations of phone number","String permutations","Anagram generation"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Confusing permutations (all elements, any order) with combinations (r elements, order doesn't matter)","Duplicate pruning: !visited[i-1] vs visited[i-1] — direction matters","Not backtracking the visited array"]

algorithms.name: Combination Sum
algorithms.analogy: Breaking a target amount using unlimited coin denominations. For each denomination, you can use it as many times as you want. The search tree: at each level, try each denomination from current index onwards (allowing reuse). Prune when remaining target goes negative. A solution is found when remaining target hits exactly 0.
algorithms.story: Combination Sum tests all three backtracking patterns: standard (Combination Sum I — reuse allowed), with duplicates (II — no reuse, deduplicate), and limited count (III — exactly k numbers, 1-9 only). The start index prevents revisiting earlier elements. Reuse is allowed by passing i (not i+1) in the recursive call.
algorithms.whenToUse: Find all combinations summing to target, coin change (all combinations), partition equal subset sum (DP is better for just checking possibility).
algorithms.howItWorks:
Combination Sum I (reuse allowed, distinct candidates):
1. If remaining == 0 → add path to result
2. If remaining < 0 → return (prune)
3. For i from start to end:
   a. Add candidates[i] to path
   b. Recurse with start=i (NOT i+1, allows reuse)
   c. Remove last element (backtrack)

Combination Sum II (no reuse, may have duplicates):
- Sort candidates
- Pass start=i+1
- Skip: if i > start && candidates[i] == candidates[i-1]
algorithms.interviewTips: The difference between Combination Sum I and II: I passes `i` (allowing reuse), II passes `i+1` (no reuse). The `break` when `candidates[i] > remaining` works because candidates are sorted — powerful early termination. For II, the duplicate skip uses `i > start` (same level) not `i > 0`.
algorithms.useCases: ["Combination Sum I/II/III (LeetCode 39/40/216)","Coin change (all combinations)","Target sum with limited numbers","Partition equal subset"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Passing i vs i+1 — reuse vs no-reuse","Forgetting to sort for the pruning break to work","Duplicate skip: i>start vs i>0","Not breaking when candidates[i] > remaining"]
algorithms.variants: ["Combination Sum I/II/III","Coin change all combinations","Letter combinations"]

algorithms.name: N-Queens
algorithms.analogy: Place n chess queens on an n×n board so no two attack each other. Queens attack along rows, columns, and both diagonals. Place one queen per row (row 1, then row 2, etc.). For each row, try each column. Before placing, check: is this column used? Is this diagonal (row-col) used? Is this anti-diagonal (row+col) used? If safe, place and recurse to next row. Backtrack by removing queen.
algorithms.story: N-Queens is the benchmark backtracking problem. The key optimization: instead of scanning the board each time, track which columns and diagonals are occupied using three sets. A queen at (r, c) occupies column c, diagonal (r-c), and anti-diagonal (r+c). Check these sets in O(1) before placing. This brings constraint checking from O(n) to O(1) per candidate.
algorithms.whenToUse: Constraint satisfaction problems, board games, placement problems where entities conflict with each other.
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Maintain sets: cols, diag (r-c), antiDiag (r+c)
2. For each row (process rows in order):
   For each column c in 0..n-1:
     If c ∈ cols OR (row-c) ∈ diag OR (row+c) ∈ antiDiag → skip
     Place queen: add c, row-c, row+c to sets
     Recurse to next row
     Remove queen: remove from sets (backtrack)
3. If row == n → record this board configuration
algorithms.interviewTips: The diagonal trick is the key insight: all cells on the same diagonal have the same (row-col) value; same anti-diagonal have the same (row+col) value. Memorize this. Using bit manipulation instead of HashSets can further optimize to O(1) space for tracking.
algorithms.useCases: ["N-Queens (LeetCode 51)","N-Queens II — count solutions (LeetCode 52)","Sudoku solver","Constraint satisfaction in scheduling"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Checking same row — not needed since we process one queen per row","Forgetting to remove from diag and antiDiag when backtracking","Using (row+col) for both diag types — they have different formulas"]
algorithms.variants: ["N-Queens II (count only)","Sudoku solver","Place knights on chessboard"]
